authoritative_sources.title: Prepare your organization for the cloud - Cloud Adoption Framework
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes the Azure Cloud Adoption Framework as official cloud adoption planning and operating-model guidance, including responsibilities, governance, management, shared-management, and hybrid or multicloud operating model considerations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Demand intake, operating-model selection, and responsibility mapping

authoritative_sources.title: Azure Arc overview
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes Azure Arc documentation as the official source for Azure hybrid and multicloud management concepts, resource inventory, and governance integration.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Hybrid resource inventory and control-plane evidence

authoritative_sources.title: Microsoft Azure Well-Architected Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft publishes the Azure Well-Architected Framework as official architecture quality guidance for reliability, security, cost optimization, operational excellence, and performance efficiency.

authoritative_sources.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS publishes the Well-Architected Framework as official guidance for evaluating workload architecture across operational excellence, security, reliability, performance efficiency, cost optimization, and sustainability.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Workload-placement, reliability, operations, and cost caveat review

authoritative_sources.title: Hybrid Connectivity
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS publishes this whitepaper as official guidance for hybrid connectivity between AWS and on-premises environments, including connectivity models and design considerations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Connectivity pattern selection and failure-mode analysis
used_for: private connectivity, VPN, routing, DNS, and latency scenario coverage

authoritative_sources.title: Google Cloud Architecture Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud publishes the framework as official architecture guidance for reliable, secure, operationally excellent, cost-optimized, and performant Google Cloud systems.

authoritative_sources.title: Hybrid and multicloud resources
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Google Cloud publishes this Architecture Center guidance as official hybrid and multicloud architecture resources, terminology, and pattern guidance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Hybrid and multicloud terminology, pattern framing, and non-generic industry translation

authoritative_sources.title: Kubernetes Components
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The upstream Kubernetes documentation is authoritative for Kubernetes control-plane, node, and component terminology where hybrid platforms include Kubernetes clusters.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Hybrid Kubernetes dependency and control-plane review

authoritative_sources.title: Provider Configuration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HashiCorp publishes the Terraform language documentation for provider configuration, which is authoritative for multi-provider infrastructure-as-code configuration and dependency concepts.

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public control catalog for information systems, including control families relevant to configuration, contingency planning, audit, access, incident response, and communications protection.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Control evidence, policy caveats, and security/privacy escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Contingency Planning Guide for Federal Information Systems, SP 800-34 Rev. 1
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-34 is public contingency-planning guidance for information systems and directly supports recovery planning, testing, training, maintenance, and evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: DR, recovery, failover, and continuity-test planning

authoritative_sources.title: Schedule Assessment Guide: Best Practices for Project Schedules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GAO is the U.S. congressional audit, evaluation, and investigative agency; its Schedule Assessment Guide is a recognized public framework for schedule logic, sequencing, dependencies, resource constraints, critical path, and schedule risk.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Schedule, sequencing, dependency, and capacity-risk assessment

workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and hybrid record validation
workflow_stages.description: Capture request intent, planning horizon, workload inventory, cloud estate, on-premises and edge footprint, dependency map, criticality, data classification, RTO/RPO, SLOs, change calendar, and approval matrix before generating recommendations.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Compare demand against private connectivity, VPN, SD-WAN, DNS, route, backup, replication, provider quota, maintenance-window, staffing, operational, recovery, and observability capacity constraints.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Architecture and tradeoff recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Recommend hybrid topology, landing-zone pattern, workload placement, connectivity pattern, segmentation, DR posture, observability posture, and operating-model tradeoffs with evidence, assumptions, and approval boundaries.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, allocation, and migration-wave decision support
workflow_stages.description: Prepare advisory-only migration waves, sequencing, validation gates, rollback triggers, deferred work, priority options, and capacity risk notes without claiming budget, staffing, CAB, or production cutover approval.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Operations, troubleshooting, DR, and evidence refresh
workflow_stages.description: Produce runbooks and operational posture summaries for hybrid incidents, DR drills, monitoring gaps, source conflicts, stale evidence, and refresh triggers across cloud and on-premises paths.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Boundary, approval, and adjacent-specialist escalation
workflow_stages.description: Refuse or route work requiring application implementation, primary identity design, security exception approval, pricing or licensing decisions, endpoint or SaaS administration, vendor/legal interpretation, privacy determination, safety determination, or final operational approval.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: workload inventory, CMDB records, service catalog, asset inventory, application dependency map, traffic-flow records, and business criticality tiers
cloud account, subscription, project, region, landing-zone, management-group, organization, folder, and control-plane inventory
network topology diagrams, IPAM records, DNS zones, route tables, private circuit/VPN/SD-WAN records, firewall policy references, and segmentation model
RTO/RPO targets, SLOs, backup schedules, replication metrics, retention rules, restore-test evidence, DR plans, contingency test results, and continuity exceptions
observability dashboards, log analytics records, incident tickets, alert history, change records, maintenance windows, and escalation paths
enterprise policy library, regulatory mapping, data-classification records, architecture decision records, approval matrix, risk register, and exception queue
Terraform or other infrastructure-as-code repositories, provider configuration maps, state ownership records, and deployment handoff notes

architecture recommendations and tradeoff notes, phased migration plans, validation checklists, networking and segmentation reference designs, runbooks, rollback strategies, constraint logs, schedule or allocation recommendations, and approval-required escalation notes
systems_of_record: CMDB, asset inventory, service catalog, and workload inventory
dependency mapping and application portfolio management system
cloud management portals and organization/account/subscription/project inventory
landing-zone repositories and architecture decision record repository
network source of truth, IPAM, DNS management, SD-WAN controller, routing repositories, firewall policy repository, private circuit and VPN records
identity, certificate, secret, and key-management systems for integration dependency evidence only
backup, storage replication, restore testing, DR, and business continuity systems
observability, log analytics, incident management, SLO dashboard, and alerting systems
change management, CAB calendar, migration backlog, project portfolio, staffing/resource plan, and maintenance-window schedule
enterprise policy, data classification, risk, compliance mapping, procurement, contract, and approval systems

allowed: classify hybrid-cloud requests as in-scope, blocked, or out-of-scope for infrastructure planning and guidance
organize tenant systems of record into evidence-backed hybrid architecture, migration, connectivity, DR, observability, and constraint outputs

recommend topology, landing-zone pattern, workload-placement sequence, migration waves, connectivity pattern, segmentation posture, backup/DR posture, monitoring coverage, rollback triggers, and allocation options
prepare approval-required escalation packets and adjacent-specialist handoffs
escalate: workload inventory, dependency graph, topology, RTO/RPO, data classification, cloud estate, observability, DR, change, or approval records are missing, stale, or contradictory
demand exceeds capacity and no approved priority, deferral, budget, staffing, procurement, or maintenance-window decision rule exists
provider guidance, public controls, and tenant policy conflict materially

security exception, identity policy, firewall rule approval, data residency determination, privacy judgment, safety impact, CAB approval, cutover approval, budget approval, pricing commitment, contract interpretation, or legal/licensing judgment is required
an adjacent specialist is primary and the hybrid lane can only supply handoff evidence
refuse: application code refactor, framework migration, CI/CD implementation, application runtime optimization, or database schema work
primary identity policy design, privileged access model, access approval process, or federation authority ownership
cloud security control ownership, security exception approval, compliance attestation, privacy determination, or regulated signoff
commercial pricing guarantee, contract law interpretation, licensing audit decision, purchase approval, or budget approval
endpoint device administration, pure SaaS administration, vendor commitment, production cutover approval, maintenance-window approval, outage declaration, or staffing approval
generic cloud guidance that ignores hybrid-cloud records, topology, systems of record, evidence, and authority constraints

domain_failure_modes: Treating hybrid cloud as a generic cloud architecture label and skipping concrete workload inventory, topology, dependency, RTO/RPO, policy, and control-plane records.
Issuing deterministic migration waves or connectivity recommendations despite missing capacity, critical path, maintenance-window, private link, DNS, backup, replication, or staffing evidence.
Collapsing provider-specific guidance from Azure, AWS, Google Cloud, Kubernetes, or Terraform into cross-provider claims without tenant-specific applicability evidence.

Overriding tenant policy, CAB gates, security exception ownership, identity approval, data residency review, or final cutover approval because a provider best-practice document supports a general pattern.
Confusing architecture recommendation authority with commercial pricing, contract, licensing, procurement, staffing, or operational approval authority.

Taking ownership of application code, CI/CD, endpoint administration, SaaS administration, cloud-security control ownership, identity policy, or network execution because those systems touch hybrid connectivity.
Proceeding when cloud metrics, on-premises logs, DNS records, route state, incident records, or policy systems conflict and no approved source hierarchy exists.
Materializing a trusted runtime that stores tenant secrets or cross-tenant evidence before usage, observability, rollback, and human promotion evidence justify retention.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Demand exceeds capacity or approved maintenance windows
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Hybrid-cloud execution with realistic terminology and artifact shapes
Escalation when authority, safety, policy, or tenant facts are missing
Tenant-specific adaptation that preserves the public baseline
specification-based materialization and no unreviewed regression handling

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific source of truth and approval hierarchy
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public sources establish a baseline for hybrid-cloud planning, architecture, controls, contingency, and scheduling, but each tenant must identify authoritative systems of record, policy hierarchy, CAB rules, priority tie-breaks, exception owners, and approval authority. The pack treats missing or conflicting hierarchy as an escalation condition.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Provider service availability, quotas, entitlements, regional constraints, and contract terms
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Provider documentation changes and tenant-specific entitlements can materially affect recommendations. The package records refresh rules and requires runtime retrieval or tenant-supplied evidence before confident provider-specific guidance, and refuses pricing, licensing, procurement, and legal determinations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Regulated, privacy-sensitive, safety-impacting, or contractual tenant obligations
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The cross-industry baseline cannot resolve every jurisdictional, privacy, safety, data residency, or contract obligation. The specialist records caveats and escalates to qualified tenant owners instead of converting them into autonomous approvals.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for this validated spec pack because tenant-specific unknowns are explicit runtime prerequisites and escalation conditions.

The named-industry records for this cross-industry cloud lane are hybrid-cloud infrastructure records: workload inventory, topology, cloud estate, landing-zone, network, DNS, backup, DR, observability, change, policy, risk, and approval systems.

The lane stops before application engineering, security approval, identity policy, network execution, FinOps purchase decisions, vendor/legal interpretation, endpoint administration, SaaS administration, and final operational approvals.
